perturbation.original_text: If IBM elects to amend the specification or the process for manufacturing Products, IBM will notify MSL of the changes in writing. MSL will promptly inform IBM of any changes to Delivery Dates, lead times, process changes, Parts requirements, Parts obsolescence, scrap, rework and any requested price changes that will result from the required changes.
perturbation.changed_text: If IBM elects to amend the specification or the process for manufacturing Goods, IBM will notify MSL of the changes in writing. MSL will promptly inform IBM of any changes to Delivery Dates, lead times, process changes, Parts requirements, Parts obsolescence, scrap, rework and any requested price changes that will result from the required changes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Products" to "Goods" creates ambiguity. The contract defines 'Products' in Section 1.14, but there's no parallel definition for 'Goods.' This raises questions on whether the obligations concerning manufacturing changes now apply to items beyond the defined Products, potentially leading to disputes. The definition of products means Parts, a MES, machine types, request for price quotation (“RPQ's”), model numbers and feature types purchased by IBM under this Agreement and as further described in the Product Attachments. By introducing Goods, it would make uncertain the enforcement of the same.
perturbation.contradicted_text: MSL will perform and manage selected manufacturing, Integration, and other Services, as well as sell Products to IBM, as stated in the Statement of Work and its Appendices, Attachments and Supplement for the Retail Store Solutions ("RS"), Global Embedded Production Solutions ("GEPS"), Finance Solutions, and other IBM business units.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Products" to "Goods" introduces ambiguity and creates a contradiction. The original text clearly defines "Products" in Section 1.14, providing certainty to the scope of manufacturing change obligations. By replacing it with "Goods," which lacks a specific definition within the contract, it broadens the scope and creates uncertainty regarding whether the obligations apply to items beyond the defined "Products." This contradicts the contradicted text, which states that MSL will sell Products to IBM as defined. The change leads to a potential conflict because the contract now lacks clarity on what constitutes a 'Good' versus a 'Product', making it difficult to enforce the obligations related to manufacturing changes.

perturbation.original_text: MSL may submit premium costs to IBM for reimbursement that are in addition to prices defined in Sections 7.1, and 7.2. Premium costs include materials, labor and other extraordinary items.
perturbation.changed_text: MSL may submit additional expenditure to IBM for reimbursement that are in addition to prices defined in Sections 7.1, and 7.2. Premium costs include materials, labor and other extraordinary items.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing “premium costs” with “additional expenditure” introduces a contradiction. While the original text defines “premium costs” by listing examples, the substituted term lacks definition, making the scope of reimbursable expenses unclear. The definition of premium costs include materials, labor and other extraordinary items, where as additional expenditure is unknown what that is, creating uncertanty for the enforecement. Also due to Section 9.0, where the defintion of Premium Cost, is the legal text reffering.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Premium costs may be incurred in order to meet Delivery Dates. a) MSL may submit premium costs to IBM for reimbursement that are in addition to prices defined in Sections 7.1, and 7.2. Premium costs include materials, labor and other extraordinary items. b) The Parties agree that [*] of all Parts price reductions will be [*] to IBM when MSL begins using and shipping the lower costs Parts. However, if a specific price reduction is the direct result of a previously IBM approved substantial engineering, design, or resourcing change by MSL, that price reduction will be [*] for a period of [*] months, then [*] of the Parts price reduction will be [*] to IBM. b) If Requirements Accuracy, as defined in Appendix 2, exceeds [*]% and if demand is beyond Supply Flexibility as defined in Section 13.2 and Appendix 4, premium costs resulting solely from unplanned demand shall be subject to reimbursement.
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly defines 'premium costs' with specific examples, allowing for a clear understanding of reimbursable expenses under Sections 7.1 and 7.2. The changed text replaces 'premium costs' with the broader term 'additional expenditure,' which lacks a defined scope. This introduces ambiguity, especially in light of Section 9.0's legal reliance on the definition of 'Premium Cost.' The contradicted text, which references 'premium costs' in the context of meeting delivery dates and unplanned demand, now becomes unclear due to the redefined terminology in the changed text, creating a contradiction about which expenses are reimbursable. Since the Misaligned Terminology definition is satisfied (multiple terms used interchangeably, conflicting obligations, and lack of clear definition), there is a clear in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The following is a list of responsibilities that will be required of MSL in order to fulfill the requirements of this Agreement. This list may not be all inclusive. MSL shall: 1) provide suitable installations, common tools and equipment, skilled human resources, and adequate warehousing facilities at all delivery points listed in the Product Attachments as MSL may need for execution of this Agreement, 2) manage, control, and execute EC's, 3) qualify processes in accordance with IBM specified criteria, 4) review and update product inventory profiles semiannually,
perturbation.changed_text: The following is a list of responsibilities that will be required of MSL in order to fulfill the requirements of this Agreement. This list may not be all inclusive. MSL shall: 1) provide suitable installations, common tools and equipment, trained personal, and adequate warehousing facilities at all delivery points listed in the Product Attachments as MSL may need for execution of this Agreement, 2) manage, control, and execute EC's, 3) qualify processes in accordance with IBM specified criteria, 4) review and update product inventory profiles semiannually,
perturbation.explanation: Changing "skilled human resources" to "trained personal" creates inconsistency. The contract uses “Employees” and “Transferred Employees” in Section 9.0, which implies a certain legal status and obligation towards these individuals. "Trained Personal", may refer to contractors or temporary workers and create a confusion within the legal sense of "Employees" in the contract. This can impact labor laws and benefits obligations, creating an enforcement problem.
perturbation.contradicted_text: a) Attachment 2 contains a list of the individuals employed by IBM at the date hereof in connection with this Agreement, including active employees and employees who are on leave of absence or sick leave (herein the "Employees"). b) MSL will make an unconditional employment offer to the Employees, listed in Attachment 2, to be effective on the Effective Date of this Agreement. The Employees who accept employment offers from MSL and who have begun their employment with MSL ("Transferred Employees") will be employed by MSL in accordance with the terms set forth below. IBM will terminate all Employees, listed in Attachment 2, at the Effective Date of this Agreement and IBM will be responsible for any and all employment related liabilities up to the Effective Date, including, but not limited to, vacation and sick time, workers compensation claims, variable compensation, and severance.
perturbation.justification: The change from "skilled human resources" to "trained personal" introduces a contradiction because the contradicted text specifically refers to "Employees" and "Transferred Employees" in the context of an employment offer from MSL to IBM's employees. The term 'trained personal' can be interpreted more broadly to include contractors or temporary workers, which undermines the specific employment relationship established in the contradicted text. This can create confusion about the legal status and obligations towards these individuals, specifically regarding labor laws and benefits, thus causing an enforcement problem and a direct contradiction.
